revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な[[部民]]。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも…」
revision.text:
'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な[[部民]]。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。奈良・平安時代に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、小学館、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[久米氏]] * [[大伴氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くめへ}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:部民]]

contributor.username: ja>壁掛成蛸は三考する葦である

revision.text:
'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な[[部民]]。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
久米部は [[中国地方]]・ [[四国地方]]を中心に、東海道沿いにも分布しており、 [[伯耆国]]・ [[美作国]] ・[[伊予国]]には久米郡が存在した（[[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]・ [[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]）。 [[岡山県]]には、現在も [[久米郡]]が存在する。一方で、従来から大伴氏が統轄していた大伴部が東国に多いこと、『古事記』では対等な立場で描かれている「久米氏」と「大伴氏」が、『書紀』などでは大伴氏に久米部が従属するかのように記述されていること、久米直氏が [[八色の姓]]でも賜姓されていないところから、久米部を管掌していた氏族が衰亡し、久米部も大伴氏に管理されるようになったことが想像される<ref>『日本書紀』（一）p382 - p383、岩波文庫、1994年</ref>。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。奈良・平安時代に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[久米氏]] * [[大伴氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くめへ}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:部民]] [[Category:久米氏]]

revision.text:
'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な職業部([[品部]])。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
久米部は [[中国地方]]・ [[四国地方]]を中心に、東海道沿いにも分布しており、 [[伯耆国]]・ [[美作国]] ・[[伊予国]]には久米郡が存在した（[[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]・ [[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]）。 [[岡山県]]には、現在も [[久米郡]]が存在する。一方で、従来から大伴氏が統轄していた大伴部が東国に多いこと、『古事記』では対等な立場で描かれている「久米氏」と「大伴氏」が、『書紀』などでは大伴氏に久米部が従属するかのように記述されていること、久米直氏が [[八色の姓]]でも賜姓されていないところから、久米部を管掌していた氏族が衰亡し、久米部も大伴氏に管理されるようになったことが想像される<ref>『日本書紀』（一）p382 - p383、岩波文庫、1994年</ref>。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。奈良・平安時代に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。

== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年

『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。奈良・平安時代に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。

'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な職業部([[品部]])。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
久米部は [[中国地方]]・ [[四国地方]]を中心に、東海道沿いにも分布しており、 [[伯耆国]]・ [[美作国]] ・[[伊予国]]には久米郡が存在した（[[久米郡 (鳥取県)]]・ [[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]）。 [[岡山県]]には、現在も [[久米郡]]が存在する。一方で、従来から大伴氏が統轄していた大伴部が東国に多いこと、『古事記』では対等な立場で描かれている「久米氏」と「大伴氏」が、『書紀』などでは大伴氏に久米部が従属するかのように記述されていること、久米直氏が [[八色の姓]]でも賜姓されていないところから、久米部を管掌していた氏族が衰亡し、久米部も大伴氏に管理されるようになったことが想像される<ref>『日本書紀』（一）p382 - p383、岩波文庫、1994年</ref>。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻・神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。[[奈良時代|奈良]]・[[平安時代]]に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[大久米命]] - [[久米氏]]（[[久米歌]]・[[久米舞]]） * [[久米御縣神社]]・[[久米寺]] * [[大伴氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くめへ}} [[Category:久米氏|*]] [[Category:部民]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Topics == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kume clan]] [[Category:Shinabe]] [[ja:久米部]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== Related Topics == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Middle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Miqqle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Miqqle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Miqqle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Miqqle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Miqqle Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. == See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999. == See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]

However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).

Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)|12=draft}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>

These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].

9 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:久米部 up to rev 98828065
revision.text:
'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な職業部([[品部]])。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
久米部は [[中国地方]]・ [[四国地方]]を中心に、東海道沿いにも分布しており、 [[伯耆国]]・ [[美作国]] ・[[伊予国]]には久米郡が存在した（[[久米郡 (鳥取県)]]・ [[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]）。 [[岡山県]]には、現在も [[久米郡]]が存在する。一方で、従来から大伴氏が統轄していた大伴部が東国に多いこと、『古事記』では対等な立場で描かれている「久米氏」と「大伴氏」が、『書紀』などでは大伴氏に久米部が従属するかのように記述されていること、久米直氏が [[八色の姓]]でも賜姓されていないところから、久米部を管掌していた氏族が衰亡し、久米部も大伴氏に管理されるようになったことが想像される<ref>『日本書紀』（一）p382 - p383、岩波文庫、1994年</ref>。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻・神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。[[奈良時代|奈良]]・[[平安時代]]に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[大久米命]] - [[久米氏]]（[[久米歌]]・[[久米舞]]） * [[久米御縣神社]]・[[久米寺]] * [[大伴氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くめへ}} [[Category:久米氏|*]] [[Category:部民]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[久米部]] to [[Kumebe]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''久米部'''（くめべ）とは、古代[[日本]]の軍事的な職業部([[品部]])。「来目部」・「久目部」とも書き、「大来目部」とも言う。
== 概要 == 本来は[[久米氏|久米直]]が[[伴造]]として管掌してきたもので、のちに久米直とともに[[大伴氏|大伴連]]の統率下に入った。中国・四国地方に分布し、宮廷の警衛・軍事にあたった。
久米部は [[中国地方]]・ [[四国地方]]を中心に、東海道沿いにも分布しており、 [[伯耆国]]・ [[美作国]] ・[[伊予国]]には久米郡が存在した（[[久米郡 (鳥取県)]]・ [[久米郡 (愛媛県)]]）。 [[岡山県]]には、現在も [[久米郡]]が存在する。一方で、従来から大伴氏が統轄していた大伴部が東国に多いこと、『古事記』では対等な立場で描かれている「久米氏」と「大伴氏」が、『書紀』などでは大伴氏に久米部が従属するかのように記述されていること、久米直氏が [[八色の姓]]でも賜姓されていないところから、久米部を管掌していた氏族が衰亡し、久米部も大伴氏に管理されるようになったことが想像される<ref>『日本書紀』（一）p382 - p383、岩波文庫、1994年</ref>。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三[[神武天皇]]、『[[古事記]]』中巻・神武天皇条に載せられている[[久米歌]]（来目歌）は、久米部の出陣や凱旋の際に、[[久米舞]]とともに歌われたものだという。[[奈良時代|奈良]]・[[平安時代]]に宮廷の歌謡として伝えられた。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『古事記』完訳日本の古典1、[[小学館]]、1983年 * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p295、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1974年 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p352、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年
== 関連項目 == * [[大久米命]] - [[久米氏]]（[[久米歌]]・[[久米舞]]） * [[久米御縣神社]]・[[久米寺]] * [[大伴氏]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くめへ}} [[Category:久米氏|*]] [[Category:部民]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kume District (Ehime)|ja|久米郡 (愛媛県)|qq=draft}}). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Kume clan]] [[Category:Shinabe]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:久米氏
revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient [[Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[Hōki Province]], [[Mimasaka Province]], and [[Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kume District, Ehime|Kume District (Ehime)]]). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kumenomiagata Shrine|Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[Ōtomo clan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
[[Category:Kume clan]] [[Category:Shinabe]]

revision.text:
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]] and [[Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[:en:Hōki Province|Hōki Province]], [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kume District, Ehime|Kume District (Ehime)]]). In [[Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>

These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[Nara period]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]] and [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[:en:Tōkaidō|Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[:en:Hōki Province|Hōki Province]], [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kume District, Ehime|Kume District (Ehime)]]). In [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kumenomiagata Shrine|Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]] and [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[:en:Tōkaidō|Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[:en:Hōki Province|Hōki Province]], [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|1=Kume district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10877702}}, [[:en:Kume District, Ehime|Kume District (Ehime)]]). In [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55526559]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo no Muraji]]. The Kume no Be was distributed across the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku]] and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]] and [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the [[:en:Tōkaidō|Tōkaidō]]. Provinces such as [[:en:Hōki Province|Hōki Province]], [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]], and [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]] had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|1=Kume district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10877702}}, [[:en:Kume District, Ehime|Kume District (Ehime)]]). In [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), [[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume no Miagata Shrine|Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}
[[ja:久米部]]

These performances were passed down as court songs in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
