revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55526559]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo no Muraji}}. The Kume no Be was distributed across the {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|WD=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku}} and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|WD=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku region}} and [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the {{ill|Tōkaidō|en|Tōkaidō|WD=Q413148|lt=Tōkaidō}}. Provinces such as {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|WD=Q1053703|lt=Hōki Province}}, {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|WD=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province}} had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|1=Kume district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10877702}}, {{ill|Kume District, Ehime|en|Kume District, Ehime|WD=Q10877703|lt=Kume District (Ehime)}}). In {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}} and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume no Miagata Shrine|Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo clan}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:久米部]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:久米部]]
'''Kume no Be''' (久米部) refers to a military occupational group ([[Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) in ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is also written as "Raikome no Be" (来目部) or "Kumeme no Be" (久目部) and sometimes referred to as "Ōkome no Be" (大来目部).
== Overview ==
Originally, the [[Kume clan|Kume no Atae]] oversaw the group as [[Tomobeyakko]], but later, along with the Kume no Atae, it came under the command of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo no Muraji|1=Ōtomo clan}}. The Kume no Be was distributed across the {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|WD=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku|1=Chūgoku region}} and [[Shikoku]] regions and served in court security and military functions.
The Kume no Be was distributed primarily in the {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|WD=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku region|1=Chūgoku region}} and [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]], but they were also found along the {{ill|Tōkaidō|en|Tōkaidō|WD=Q413148|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō}}. Provinces such as {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|WD=Q1053703|lt=Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}, {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}, and {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|WD=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}} had districts named Kume (e.g., {{ill|Kume District (Tottori)|ja|久米郡 (鳥取県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume District (Tottori)|1=Kume district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10877702}}, {{ill|Kume District, Ehime|en|Kume District, Ehime|WD=Q10877703|lt=Kume District (Ehime)|1=Kume District, Ehime}}). In {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, the [[Kume District]] still exists today.
However, several indicators suggest a decline in the clan responsible for overseeing the Kume no Be, leading to their eventual subordination to the Ōtomo clan. These include: - The predominance of Ōtomo-associated occupational groups ([[Ōtomo no Be]]) in the eastern provinces. - The ''Kojiki'' presenting the Kume and Ōtomo clans as equals, whereas the ''Nihon Shoki'' depicts the Kume no Be as subordinate to the Ōtomo clan. - The lack of noble titles awarded to the Kume no Atae clan during the [[Yakusa no Kabane|Yatsukusa no Kabane reform]]. <ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Volume I, pp. 382-383, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994.</ref>
The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (Volume III) under {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} and the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (Mi11le Volume) record the ''[[Kume no Uta]]'' (久米歌), which was sung alongside the [[Kume no Mai]] dance during deployments or triumphant returns by the Kume no Be. These performances were passed down as court songs in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999.
== See Also == * [[Ōkume no Mikoto]] - [[Kume clan]] (''[[Kume no Uta]]'', ''[[Kume no Mai]]'') * [[Kume no Miagata Shrine|Kume Miyanaka Shrine]] and [[Kume-dera Temple]] * {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|久米部|version=98828065|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:久米部]]

== Notes ==
{{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volume I), [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1994. * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Complete Modern Translation (Upper Volume), [[Kodansha Academic Bunko]], translated by [[Mitsuo Ujitani]], 1988. * ''Kojiki,'' Complete Translation of Japan's Ancient Classics 1, [[Shogakukan]], 1983. * ''Kadokawa Second Edition Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 295, [[Mitsutaka Takayanagi]] and [[Tadakazu Takeuchi]] (editors), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1974. * ''Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary,'' p. 352, supervised by [[Keiji Nagahara]], {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1999.
